item.title: ‘From bush materials to bricks’: Lai Valley secondary school dream nears reality
item.description: What began as a community effort with bush-material classrooms in 2013 is now taking shape as a permanent secondary school in Lai Valley LLG. The Lai Valley United Church Technical Secondary School is nearing completion of a state-of-the-art new multipurpose building, funded by the Mendi Munihu District Development Authority [MM DDA]. The steel and brick structure will be opened in December, in ti
item.content:encoded:
What began as a community effort with bush-material classrooms in 2013 is now taking shape as a permanent secondary school in Lai Valley LLG.

The Lai Valley United Church Technical Secondary School is nearing completion of a state-of-the-art new multipurpose building, funded by the Mendi Munihu District Development Authority [MM DDA]. The steel and brick structure will be opened in December, in time for the school’s first Grade 12 graduation.

The project traces back to December 2013, when Governor William Powi announced K600,000 for the new high school. 16 hectares of land was donated by the United Church and local landowners to make it happen.

Local MP Raphael Tonpi said the new multipurpose building, constructed with steel and brick, is designed to last for many years.

"Such permanent structures boost confidence among students and contribute to improved academic performance. The building is the first of its kind with plans to extend similar construction to other schools in the district," he said.

He said the new developments will address the long-standing problem of students having to travel long distances and live in squatter settlements in Mendi.

“In the district, we have the pioneer Mogol and Mendi Secondary Schools, and then several junior high schools were established including Lai Valley United Church Secondary,” he said.

Tonpi, an educationist and former headmaster of Kip Primary School, also chaired the newly established Lai Valley United Church High School before resigning to contest the Mendi-Munihu Open Seat in 2017 National General Election. He lost that election but won the seat in 2022 and has since prioritized education in the district.

He credited the Lai Valley community for volunteering labour when work started in 2013.

“The hard work of the Lai Valley community who took the initiative to work for free in 2013 are now reaping what they have sown,” he said.

Students selected to Mendi Day Secondary School have previously faced accommodation challenges.

“Those with relatives were lucky, while those without had to squeeze their children into squatter settlements, which affected learning. Town means money, and parents could not afford food or other essentials every day,” he said.

He said Mendi Munihu DDA will continue to prioritize education, calling it key to transforming the district and the nation.

item.title: ‘We believe in you’: Kumura challenges subsidized students to rebuild Usino-Bundi
item.description: Usino-Bundi MP Vincent Kumura, has delivered a blunt message to tertiary students receiving district school fee subsidies: 'the local government is investing heavily in your minds, and you have a clear duty to bring that knowledge back home'. The District Development Authority, has officially begun rolling out its K1.5 million Tuition Assistance Program for 2026, disbursing a total of K150,000 yes
item.content:encoded:
Usino-Bundi MP Vincent Kumura, has delivered a blunt message to tertiary students receiving district school fee subsidies: 'the local government is investing heavily in your minds, and you have a clear duty to bring that knowledge back home'.

The District Development Authority, has officially begun rolling out its K1.5 million Tuition Assistance Program for 2026, disbursing a total of K150,000 yesterday across Madang’s major tertiary institutions. The funding leg included Divine Word University [DWU], the PNG Maritime College, Madang Teachers College, and Madang Technical College. Under the district’s co-funding policy, the DDA is absorbing 40 percent of the student fee burden, while parents meet the remaining 60 percent.

Taking time out of his busy schedule to meet face-to-face with the student body at DWU, Kumura said funding is not a standard political handout, but a calculated investment in the district's future development capacity.

"Amidst my busy schedule, I have to find time to come out and meet you to remind you that you have a duty to your district," Kumura challenged the students. "But the onus and the responsibility is on you. We trust you, and we believe in you. That's why we are investing in your education. We trust that you will go back to the district and help develop it with the knowledge you will gain from your studies."

Of the total K1.5 million budgetary allocation, K1 million has been locked in for long-form tertiary institutions, while K500,000 is earmarked for specialized short courses. The nationwide rollout began last week targeting tertiary institutions in Eastern Highlands and Morobe provinces before expanding to Madang this week.

Kumura said the tuition assistance was a strategic 'little push or extra push' to complement the immense financial sacrifices already made by parents on the ground.

He left the students with stern, practical lifestyle advice, urging the future professionals to choose their friends wisely, protect their academic pathways, and maintain strict personal discipline.

"Always do the right thing at the right place, and at the right time," Kumura told them, reiterating that they can never go wrong when maintaining that focus. "We are investing in your education because we believe in you."

item.title: Inter-agency coalition sworn-in to clean up Milne Bay business environment
item.description: A critical alliance of provincial administrators, municipal leaders, and national tax and labor watchdogs has been formalized in Alotau to restore order to Milne Bay's commercial sector. Supported directly by the Provincial Executive Council [PEC], the newly sworn-in Provincial Economic Facilitation Committee [EFC] brings an abrupt end to years of fragmented business oversight that allowed unregul
item.content:encoded:
A critical alliance of provincial administrators, municipal leaders, and national tax and labor watchdogs has been formalized in Alotau to restore order to Milne Bay's commercial sector.

Supported directly by the Provincial Executive Council [PEC], the newly sworn-in Provincial Economic Facilitation Committee [EFC] brings an abrupt end to years of fragmented business oversight that allowed unregulated investments to proliferate across the region unchecked.

The multi-year absence of this committee created a wide regulatory vacuum, effectively leaving an open door for outside interests to establish businesses within Milne Bay without rigorous screening or monitoring.

The broad makeup of the newly inaugurated team is an aggressive shift towards unified enforcement. Led by Alotau Town Mayor and PEC Chairman for Commerce and Tourism, Esmond Basinauro, the nine-member committee merges local knowledge with national enforcement muscle.

The body pulls in ex-officio heads from Provincial Lands, Commerce & Industry, Local Level Government [LLG] Affairs, and Law & Order, alongside the Alotau Urban Council, the Milne Bay Chamber of Commerce, the Internal Revenue Commission [IRC], and the Department of Labour and Industrial Relations.

Acting Provincial Administrator Sharon Mua, who formally witnessed the milestone ceremony, voiced deep gratitude for the collaborative step.

"We have stayed away for such a long time and I am grateful that our PEC, with Chairman Esmond Basinauro, has taken that initiative and step to bring back this important committee in place again for the province," Mua said.

Originally established in the early 2000s and reinforced by the Economic Facilitation Committee Law 2017, the EFC's core mandate is to screen for genuine business ventures, hold corporations accountable to local laws, protect employees, and collect compliance penalties and levy fees to drive internal revenue.

Deputy Chairman and Principal Advisor for Commerce, Harry Sioni, said the immediate priority of the coalition will be active field deployments into rural districts and LLGs to cross-examine active investors.

"We have investors that have come into the province and are operating in the districts and LLGs, a lot of them," Sioni said. "We have not been going out to monitor their activities on whether they are abiding by their stated business scope or are doing something else, which is most times the case when left unchecked. This committee is for this exact purpose."

With the full backing of the provincial government, the newly established inter-agency coalition is now fully empowered to ensure that citizen welfare and statutory compliance remain at the heart of Milne Bay's economic landscape.

item.title: Air Niugini modernizes Wewak presence with new high-traffic sales hub
item.description: Air Niugini has taken a significant step towards elevating its customer service standards in East Sepik Province, officially launching a modern retail sales outlet in Wewak today. The national carrier has strategically relocated its regional sales operations to Tang Mow Plaza, positioning its services inside one of the town’s primary commercial centers to ensure maximum visibility and seamless wal
item.content:encoded:
Air Niugini has taken a significant step towards elevating its customer service standards in East Sepik Province, officially launching a modern retail sales outlet in Wewak today.

The national carrier has strategically relocated its regional sales operations to Tang Mow Plaza, positioning its services inside one of the town’s primary commercial centers to ensure maximum visibility and seamless walk-in access for everyday passengers.

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird when officiating the opening ceremony, welcomed the corporate upgrade, saying the investment represents a highly anticipated win for local commuters who rely heavily on air travel as an economic lifeline.

He commended the national airline for strengthening its commercial presence, stating that robust corporate infrastructure is vital for keeping East Sepik connected to the rest of Papua New Guinea and international markets.

The new facility replaces older retail constraints with a spacious, modernized corporate layout. The upgrade will allow ticketing personnel to more efficiently handle passenger inquiries, manage complex travel itineraries, process group bookings, and deliver face-to-face customer support.

Air Niugini’s management indicated that setting up operations inside the busy plaza complex will significantly reduce transit friction for provincial travelers looking to secure flights and manage travel profiles before heading out to Boram Airport.

item.title: Treasury to step in after provincial failures leave teachers without fares
item.description: The national government is stepping in to bypass provincial bureaucratic bottlenecks that have left thousands of educators stranded without their legally mandated travel entitlements. Teaching Service Commission [TSC] Chairman Maini Ugaia announced a major administrative directive on Wednesday, confirming that teachers who were short-changed or completely missed out on their 2025 and 2026 leave fa
item.content:encoded:
The national government is stepping in to bypass provincial bureaucratic bottlenecks that have left thousands of educators stranded without their legally mandated travel entitlements.

Teaching Service Commission [TSC] Chairman Maini Ugaia announced a major administrative directive on Wednesday, confirming that teachers who were short-changed or completely missed out on their 2025 and 2026 leave fares can now claim their entitlements through a newly consolidated national pathway.

Traditionally, leave fare funding is decentralized and dispatched directly to provincial governments for local distribution. However, Chairman Ugaia admitted that this system has chronically failed many on the ground, resulting in some teachers receiving only partial payments or missing out on their allocations entirely.

The TSC will now bypass the provincial financial roadblocks to remedy this. Under the new directive, affected teachers must immediately compile and submit all necessary supporting documentation to their local Provincial TSC Advisors and Provincial Education Advisors [PEAs].

"Upon receiving and verifying these files, the TSC will consolidate the data and submit the claims directly to the National Department of Treasury to facilitate prompt budget clearance and payment," Chairman Ugaia confirmed.

Ugaia said this swift administrative intervention comes with the direct backing of Prime Minister James Marape, who is actively pushing for the resolution of all outstanding teacher entitlements to maintain critical stability and morale within the national education sector.

The TSC Chairman is now urging provincial education authorities to act with extreme speed to verify and forward the outstanding files so that teachers can receive their entitlements without further delay.

item.title: TSC orders immediate salary restoration for 8,000 suspended teachers
item.description: The Teaching Service Commission [TSC] has ordered the immediate reinstatement of salaries for approximately 8,000 teachers affected by an automatic payroll suspension on Pay 10. TSC acting chairman Maini Ugaia has issued an urgent directive to all Provincial TSC Advisors and Provincial Education Advisors nationwide to rapidly collect and submit outstanding Resumption of Duty Summary Sheets to the
item.content:encoded:
The Teaching Service Commission [TSC] has ordered the immediate reinstatement of salaries for approximately 8,000 teachers affected by an automatic payroll suspension on Pay 10.

TSC acting chairman Maini Ugaia has issued an urgent directive to all Provincial TSC Advisors and Provincial Education Advisors nationwide to rapidly collect and submit outstanding Resumption of Duty Summary Sheets to the headquarters to fast-track the payroll restoration.

The mass suspension was originally scheduled to take effect on Pay 7. However, Chairman Ugaia revealed that the commission deferred the exercise after initial data indicated a staggering 29,000 teachers were at risk of being cut off nationwide. Ongoing data verification and system updates successfully whittled that backlog down to the 8,000 who were ultimately affected.

"The Commission remains deeply committed to teacher welfare," Ugaia stated, urging immediate compliance from provincial administrative offices to minimize further financial hardship on the affected educators and their families.

In a significant humanitarian move, the TSC confirmed that teachers stationed in provinces heavily impacted by the recent Cyclone Maila have been completely exempted from the payroll audit, with their salaries deliberately maintained to support local recovery efforts.

Provincial education officers have been told to take immediate action, gather the missing data from the ground, and dispatch verified data sheets to the national headquarters in Port Moresby immediately to facilitate prompt back-pay processing.

item.title: JPHA localizes payroll: CEO warns workforce to shape up or face pay cuts
item.description: The Jiwaka Provincial Health Authority [JPHA] is clamping down on workforce truancy and administrative inefficiency by taking full local control of its financial operations. Following intensive technical training conducted by the national Department of Personnel Management [DPM], the authority is set to implement the Alesco Payroll System, effectively moving administrative powers out of Waigani an
item.content:encoded:
The Jiwaka Provincial Health Authority [JPHA] is clamping down on workforce truancy and administrative inefficiency by taking full local control of its financial operations.

Following intensive technical training conducted by the national Department of Personnel Management [DPM], the authority is set to implement the Alesco Payroll System, effectively moving administrative powers out of Waigani and directly into the province.

JPHA Chief Executive Officer Thaddeus Turi made it clear that localizing the system is not just an administrative upgrade but a critical tool for strict workforce accountability and performance tracking.

With the digital system active on the ground in Jiwaka, executive management will now have the immediate regulatory authority to monitor, scrutinize, and directly freeze or cease the salaries of non-compliant employees.

"The power to cease salaries is now on hand," CEO Turi said during the training's closing ceremony on Friday, May 15th. "All workforce members must now get their job descriptions (JD) and terms of reference (TOR) in order, and work effectively according to National Health plans and targets."

Previously, resolving default payroll issues or punishing underperforming staff required costly, time-consuming trips to central government offices in Port Moresby. Turi said those expensive logistical hurdles is a thing of the past now.

The training facilitator from DPM, Waire Era, explained that provincial participants underwent rigorous instruction covering governance, dashboard operations, pay cycles, system data forms, and data entry. She confirmed that DPM will soon invite the participants to Port Moresby for final, hands-on practical training and formal certification.

"After certifying the participants, the DPM will give the access to run their own Alesco payroll system," Era said. "That is why we are saying that we are bringing the Waigani office into JPHA."

According to CEO Turi, the Alesco integration represents the final puzzle piece for JPHA’s modernization strategy. The system seamlessly links with the authority's existing Integrated Financial Management System [IFMS] and upgraded digital ICT infrastructure to create a fully modern public service network.

"JPHA has come a long way, facing a lot of challenges," Turi said. "We have been working very hard to create an effective working system and a complete workforce. This installation will fully complete the JPHA Health System."

item.title: PNG Aviation spared from global jet fuel crisis, watchdog confirms sufficient stock
item.description: While global airlines slash daily flight schedules, spike airfares, and seek massive emergency bailouts amid a highly volatile international aviation fuel crisis, Papua New Guinea’s domestic aviation industry remains remarkably insulated. The Independent Consumer and Competition Commission [ICCC] has confirmed that the country currently holds sufficient stockpiles of Jet A1 fuel, successfully aver
item.content:encoded:
While global airlines slash daily flight schedules, spike airfares, and seek massive emergency bailouts amid a highly volatile international aviation fuel crisis, Papua New Guinea’s domestic aviation industry remains remarkably insulated.

The Independent Consumer and Competition Commission [ICCC] has confirmed that the country currently holds sufficient stockpiles of Jet A1 fuel, successfully averting a potential transport crisis that could have paralyzed the national economy.

The stabilization brings critical relief to a nation heavily reliant on regional air networks. Because the Papua New Guinea mainland is largely road-locked and geographically isolated from scattered island provinces, air transport serves as an absolute lifeline, connecting millions of citizens and cargo routes to the capital, Port Moresby. Any sudden shortage of aviation fuel among domestic operators would trigger a immediate, devastating ripple effect across domestic commerce, public travel, and medical supply chains.

"In terms of our consultations with relevant stakeholders and also industry players, we have been informed that for Jet A1 fuel, there is enough stock in the market," ICCC Commissioner Roy Daggy stated in response to questions from NBC News in Port Moresby on Tuesday.

"So there is no challenge… fuel distributors say there are enough fuel stocks in the market," the head of the consumer and competition watchdog said.

While Commissioner Daggy stopped short of disclosing the exact duration of the country's current aviation fuel reserves, the regulatory assurance provides major breathing room for local commercial operators. The ongoing global fuel crunch has been fueled by limited supply on the international market, a continued aftershock of escalating conflicts in the Middle East.

Papua New Guinea's domestic shield against these imported price shocks is backed by direct government intervention. The ICCC sits as a core member of the High-Level State Taskforce, which was established by the National Government to implement its aggressive fiscal policies. The taskforce remains actively engaged in subsidizing fuel prices to cushion the domestic economy from the inflationary impacts of refined petroleum imports.

item.title: Solar lights boost church services in Nipa Kutubu
item.description: Pastors in remote parts of Nembi Plateau LLG are set to improve night services and prepare sermons after receiving solar lights from community leader Fiebik Kilip. The presentations were done at Topua Village. For years, congregations across the area have struggled to hold evening fellowships and worship services due to the lack of electricity. Many pastors relied on candles and small torches to c
item.content:encoded:
Pastors in remote parts of Nembi Plateau LLG are set to improve night services and prepare sermons after receiving solar lights from community leader Fiebik Kilip. The presentations were done at Topua Village.

For years, congregations across the area have struggled to hold evening fellowships and worship services due to the lack of electricity. Many pastors relied on candles and small torches to continue preaching.

Kilip, the former Southern Highlands Provincial Deputy Provincial Administrator for Economic and Social Services, said the donation was aimed at supporting churches that have long filled service gaps where government presence is limited.

“Churches have played a vital role in rural areas by establishing schools, health facilities, roads, and most importantly, by preaching the Gospel,” he said. “In many places where government services have not reached, the church is already there.”

Kilip said the support would help strengthen night services, improve fellowship, and allow churches to continue spreading the Gospel more effectively.

“Partnership between churches, government, and the people is very important,” he said. “When these three work together, communities become peaceful, stable, and developed.”

He added that churches also play a key role in changing mindsets and promoting respect for law and order.

Pastor Dickson Nom of Topua PNG Bible Church thanked Mr. Kilip on behalf of the local churches, describing the assistance as the first of its kind in the area. He said the lack of lighting had been a real challenge for churches, and acknowledged that God had provided relief through Mr. Kilip’s support.

item.title: K3m spark for Vanimo Free Trade Zone, but massive infrastructure hurdles remain
item.description: A 20-year political promise moved a step closer to fruition as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone [FTZ] secured a K3 million operational startup fund from the Vanimo-Green District Development Authority [DDA]. Yet, despite local MP Belden Namah declaring the project a reality and no longer a dream, the ambitious trade hub faces a staggering checklist of missing critical infrastructure before it can succes
item.content:encoded:
A 20-year political promise moved a step closer to fruition as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone [FTZ] secured a K3 million operational startup fund from the Vanimo-Green District Development Authority [DDA].

Yet, despite local MP Belden Namah declaring the project a reality and no longer a dream, the ambitious trade hub faces a staggering checklist of missing critical infrastructure before it can successfully compete on the global stage.

The project, newly incorporated with the Investment Promotion Authority [IPA] as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone Holdings Limited, is designed to turn West Sepik into a manufacturing and logistics powerhouse by leveraging its proximity to neighboring Indonesia.

However, for the trade zone to achieve full operational capacity, Vanimo desperately lacks the basic foundations of an international trading hub. To attract foreign investment, the project requires an international-standard airport, an upgraded shipping wharf, a secure land mass for an export processing zone, and a streamlined, fully functional border post at Wutung.

Furthermore, the local manufacturing sector remains heavily handcuffed by PNG’s unstable provincial power and water grid. Minister for Tourism, Arts, and Culture, Belden Namah, who has led the project since its initial conception, acknowledged these gaps, stating he is aggressively lobbying the National Government to step in.

“I am pushing hard to the National Government for Vanimo to have an International Airport and an International Sea Port," Namah said. "I am also hoping that the National Government fast-tracks the rollout of the province having access to the excess power supply from Indonesia.”

First conceptualized over two decades ago by former West Sepik Governor John Tekwi, the economic zone has long been viewed as the country's best shot at diversifying away from resource extraction and into downstream manufacturing.

“This initiative has the potential to transform not only the Vanimo-Green electorate but the entire West Sepik Province," the minister said. "Let me assure you today, it is no longer a dream. The Vanimo Free Trade Zone is here to stay.”

While Namah credited the Marape-Rosso government's policy framework for creating the pathway to bring this long-term vision to life, locals say that the true test of the Vanimo FTZ will not be this initial K3 million startup capital. Instead, the survival of the project depends on whether Port Moresby will commit the hundreds of millions required to build the transport and utility networks needed to sustain it.
